title: ADMP Regulatory Trigger
description: ADMP regulation only applies where the use of automated decision-making (or profiling) results in legal effects concerning the data subject or significantly affects the data subject. Source: JPDP ADMP Guideline (PCP No. 3/2025 substantive content).

trigger.rule: Automated decision-making and profiling are regulated only if their use results in legal effects concerning the data subject OR significantly affects the data subject.
twoConditions: Decisions are being made by technological means without human involvement; AND
Such decisions may legally or significantly affect an individual.

legalEffects.definition: Something that affects a person's legal status or their legal rights.
examples: Denial of social benefit granted by law

significantlyAffects.definition: Produces an effect that is equivalent or similarly significant in its impact.
examples: Automatic refusal of an online credit application without human intervention
Automated e-recruiting practices without human intervention
decisionMustHavePotentialTo: Significantly affect the circumstances, behaviour or choices of the individuals concerned
Have a prolonged or permanent impact on the data subject
At its most extreme, lead to the exclusion or discrimination of individuals
exampleDomains: Decisions affecting access to health services
Decisions denying employment opportunity or causing serious disadvantage
Decisions resulting in price discrimination between data subjects
Decisions affecting access to education (e.g., university admissions)

outsideTrigger.rule: ADMP that has no real impact on the individual is not regulated.
examples: Researchers using automated profiling for sociological studies of city vs suburban residents (general trend analysis, no individual impact)
Customer 'stickiness' analysis used to improve services overall (not discriminating between user categories)

scopingFlow: 1. Identify all processing operations involving automated decisions or profiling
2. For each, assess whether it is solely automated (no human in the loop) — if a human only inputs data and the system decides, it remains 'solely automated'
3. For each solely-automated operation, assess whether it produces legal effects OR significantly affects the data subject
4. If yes to both: ADM Restrictions apply (Right to Refuse, Right to Information, Right to Human Review)
5. If only Right to Information applies under an Exception, transparency is still mandatory
6. Cross-check DPIA threshold — ADM processing involving >10,000 subjects also triggers a mandatory DPIA
